coredata.dc:title: Universal Hall conductivity in graphene Maxwell fish-eye quantum dot
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Gevorkian, Zhyrair
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dot with Maxwell fish eye potential energy profile is studied. A quasiclassical approximation is used given that potential energy is a slowly varying function of coordinates. Near the zero energy the spectrum of electron macroscopically degenerates. All the electron trajectories are closed circles that are classified by angular momentum and an additional integral of motion. Provided the complete filling of the lower Dirac zone, a universal value for Hall conductivity is found.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dot
Maxwell fish-eye
Hall conductivity

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
Energy dependence on orbital quantum number
j > 0
.For
j < 0
, one has the symmetrical picture.
The only contribution to Hall conductivity gives basic trajectory (red), contributions from paired (blue) trajectories cancel to each other.

head.ce:title: Universal Hall conductivity in graphene Maxwell fish-eye quantum dot

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yerevan Physics Institute, Alikhanian Brothers St. 2, 0036 Yerevan, Armenia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yerevan Physics Institute,Alikhanian Brothers St. 2,0036 Yerevan, Armenia

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Radiophysics and Electronics, Ashtarak-2, 0203, Armenia

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Yerevan Physics Institute, Alikhanian Brothers St. 2, 0036 Yerevan, Armenia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dot with Maxwell fish eye potential energy profile is studied. A quasiclassical approximation is used given that potential energy is a slowly varying function of coordinates. Near the zero energy the spectrum of electron macroscopically degenerates. All the electron trajectories are closed circles that are classified by angular momentum and an additional integral of motion. Provided the complete filling of the lower Dirac zone, a universal value for Hall conductivity is found.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQD) attract significant interest due to numerous applications [1]. One of the most widely-used properties of GQDs is photoluminescence, which is the emission of light by graphene when after absorption of the incident photons, excited electrons within the GQD relax back to the lower energy levels. The Pl properties depend on the energy spectrum of electrons in the GQD. The energy spectrum itself is determined by the potential energy profile. Different potential energy profiles for graphene quantum dots were considered including Coulomb [2] and other profiles [3]. Main focus of these studies was the energy spectrum. In the present paper we investigate the Maxwell fish eye profile which is widely used in optics [4,5]. It was shown [6] that in this profile in the geometrical approximation limit the closed circular trajectories of the photon can be classified by the integrals of motion. In this paper, we apply these results to the electrons in graphene that are described by the massless Dirac equation.
Different types of Hall currents were considered in graphene. The conventional type of current is associated to the drift of cyclotron orbits of electron in perpendicular magnetic field [7].

Topological Hall current due to Berry phase and curvature of graphene bands takes place without external magnetic field [8]. In contrast to conventional case this current is neutral because the contributions of
K
′
and K valleys have opposite signs.

In the present paper, we consider another type of Hall current that does not require a magnetic field. It appears because of the drift of circular orbits due to the Maxwell fish-eye potential energy profile. This current originates due to the interaction of the pseudospin of graphene electron and the inhomogeneity.

We start from the Dirac equation for the electrons in graphene [7]
(1)
[ − i
v
F
σ ⋅ ∇ +
( V
( r )
− E )
I
ˆ
]
ψ
ˆ
= 0
where
σ ≡
σ
x
,
σ
y
are two dimensional Pauli matrices,
I
ˆ
is a unity matrix, E is the energy and
V
( r )
is the external potential that is assumed spherical symmetric. Assume that the potential energy is slow varying function of coordinates. Consequently one can use quasiclassical approximation and therefore make the substitution
ψ
ˆ
=
ψ
p
ˆ
e
i pr
(2)
[
v
F
σ p +
( V
( r )
− E )
I
ˆ
]
ψ
p
ˆ
= 0
Dispersion equation follows from Eq. (2)
(3)
d e t

v

= 0
This equation has two solutions describing two branches of the Dirac cone [7]
(4)
E = −
v
F
p + V
( r )
E =
v
F
p + V
( r )
If the transitions between two zones are neglected one can introduce “weak zero” [9] Hamiltonian to describe the processes in each zone separately
(5)
H
I
= −
v
F
p + V
( r )
− E ≈ 0
For certainty let us consider the lower branch with the first Hamiltonian. We assume that the potential energy profile in quantum dot has the form of Maxwell fish eye within the accuracy of a constant number [4]
(6)
V
( r )
− E =
2
V
0
1 + α
r
2
With this substitution the problem becomes completely analogous to the problem of a photon in an inhomogeneous medium with Maxwell fish eye refraction index profile [4,5]. We will use all the results already obtained for the photon. It is well-known that [6] in this case along with the angular momentum an additional integral of motion exists
(7)
L = r × p ,
T =
( 1 − α

2

As a result of high symmetry, one can be convinced that

2
+ 4 α
L
2

4

0
2

F
2
Extended symmetry allows to obtain the particle trajectories without solving the equations of motion Eq. (7). Namely, the vector product of T and r immediately yields the expression of trajectories,
(10)

2

As it follows from Eq. (10) the electron trajectories are circles in the
x y
plane with center a and radius R , angular momentum is directed along z , T lies in the plane xy and
T ⊥ L
. It follows from Eq. (9) that there is a trajectory with maximal angular momentum
L

. Both signs lead to the same energy

. One can prove that the energy of every trajectory is also equal to zero. Substituting T and L into the Casimir condition Eq. (9), one has
(11)

2

0
1 + α
r
2
The correspondingly energy on the trajectory is

( r )
= 0
. Therefore the state
E = 0
is multiply degenerated (see below). This is analogous to the famous [10] extra
n
2
degeneracy in Coulomb problem at
E → 0 ,
n → ∞
, (see below). To estimate the degeneracy rate note that maximal angular momentum is
p

and the quantization step for angular momentum is ħ .

The coefficient 2 in Eq. (12) stands for two signs of momentum. Note that in a finite system
V
( r )
> 0
and therefore the electron energy in the higher graphene zone
E =
v
F
p + V
( r )
is always positive and therefore there is a gap between lower and higher zones.

. It follows from Eq. (17) that the potential energy violates the symmetry between holes and electrons [12] because the potential energy term

Here, we assumed
j > 0
, however calculations for
j < 0
can be carried out in an analogous manner. At
ɛ = 1 / 2 , 3 / 2
integrals diverge, however the divergence of the second integral is stronger therefore
ɛ
( 1 / 2 , 3 / 2 )
= 0
. For the other values of j we evaluate integrals numerically. The results are shown in Fig. 1

As it is seen from Fig. 1 there is a gap between macroscopically degenerated
ɛ = 0
state and non-degenerate positive energy states. In the next section we will consider the kinetic properties of the system provided that Fermi energy lies in this gap.

It is obvious from the previous section that quantum consideration leads to the same result, namely the macroscopical degeneracy of the state
E = 0
. Quantum Hamiltonian will originate other deeper
E < 0
states that our quasiclassical approach does not capture. However, they will not contribute to the linear response of the graphene electron gas to the static electric field provided that Fermi energy is at
E
F
= 0
.

The current density is determined as
(22)

= −

Here the coefficient 4 stands for spin and valley degeneracy, f is the distribution function that should be found from the kinetic equation. The equilibrium distribution function at
t = − ∞
has the form
(23)
f
( r , p , t = − ∞ )

p − V
( r )
)

Here we assume that the Fermi energy lies in the lower branch and thus do not consider the transitions between the Dirac cones. Afterwards, according to the linear response theory, one adiabatically switches on the electrical field that reaches its constant value at t to induce the current. The equations of motion have the form

In

in Eq. (28), electric field in linear approximation can be put
E
( t )
= 0
. Therefore the unperturbed circles Eq. (10) that ensure the zeros of the argument of delta function can give contribution to the integral on r . Each circle has its counterpart that differs by the sign of y and these opposite circles cancel each others contribution to j , see Fig. 2. Therefore the only contribution to the integral comes from the basic trajectory (see above) with the center at
a = 0
and radius
r ≡
r
0
that has not a counterpart, see Fig. 2.

Hence taking into account the property of delta function with complex function in argument, the current density can be rewritten (see Fig. 2)

Subscript zero means the coordinates and momentums on the basic trajectory. The current density Eq. (29) is the instantaneous value at a given time t . However, the physical meaning has the averaged over a period of motion in a basic circle. Before that by taking into account that
E
( τ )

Substituting Eq. (32) into Eq. (30) and taking the integral, for the Hall conductivity one has
(33)

=
e
2

1
S
∑
p
The quantity
N =
1
S
∑
p
consists of two parts: the number of all states at zero energy i.e. the degeneracy rate Eq. (12) and the number of p within a closed trajectory with given angular momentum
N =
N
d
N

. The number we calculate for the basic trajectory with maximal angular momentum
p
0

(34)
N

S
4 π

0
2
Substituting Eqs. (12) and (34) into Eq. (33), we finally obtain
(35)
σ
x y
=
e
2
h
In the same conditions of graphene electron gas one, can be convinced that diagonal terms of conductivity tensor are equal to zero:
σ
x x
=
σ
y y
= 0
. The appearance of nonzero Hall conductivity in the absence of external magnetic field is a result of interaction of graphene electron pseudospin and inhomogeneity. The latter is analogous to spin Hall effects in optics [15] and condensed matter [16].

We have considered the spectrum and kinetic properties of a graphene quantum dot with Maxwell fish-eye potential energy profile. The quasiclassical approximation is used which is correct provided the electron de Broglie wavelength is much smaller than the characteristic radius of the potential energy profile
ħ
v
F
/
V
0
≪
r
0
. The problem is reduced to the motion of a massless particle in a Maxwell fish-eye potential energy profile. Because of the existence of an additional integral of motion, the circular trajectories of the particle are found without solving the equations of motion. It is shown that the
E = 0
state is macroscopically degenerated. Hall conductivity acquires a universal value
σ
H
=
e
2
/ h
provided that Fermi energy is zero.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Armenian Science Committee projects 20RF-023 and 21AG-1C062.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene quantum dots and their applications in bioimaging, biosensing, and therapy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Seokhwan Chung, Richard A. Revia, and Miqin Zhang,Graphene Quantum Dots and Their Applications in Bioimaging, Biosensing, and Therapy,Adv. Mater. 1904362, (2019), DOI: 10.1002/adma.201904362.

Vitor M. Pereira, Johan Nilsson, and A. H. Castro Neto, Phys.Rev.Lett. 99, 166802 (2007) A. V. Shytov, M. I. Katsnelson, L. S. Levitov,Phys. Rev. Lett. 99, 236801 (2007); D. S. Novikov,Phys. Rev. B 76, 245435, (2007); C. M. Lee and K. S. Chan,Journal of Applied Physics 114, 143708 (2013);
Hong-Yi Chen, Vadim Apalkov, and Tapash Chakraborty, Phys.Rev.Lett. 98, 186803 (2007).
M. Born and E. Wolf, Principles of Optics, 4th ed., (Pergamon Press, Oxford, 1970).
U. Leonhardt, Science 312, 1777 (2006); New J. Phys. 8, 118 (2006).
A. H. Castro Neto ,F. Guinea,N. M. R. Peres, K. S. Novoselov and A. K. Geim,Rev. Mod. Phys. 81, 109 (2009).
R. V. Gorbachev, J. C. W. Song, G. L. Yu, A. V. Kretinin, et al Science, 346(6208),448-451,(2014).doi:10.1126/science.1254966.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: J. Opt. A: Pure Appl. Opt.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.Bliokh,J. Opt. A: Pure Appl. Opt.11,094009 (2009).
L. D. Landau and E. M. Lifshitz, Quantum Mechanics:Non-Relativistic Theory (Pergamon, New York), 1981.
D. P. DiVincenzo et al., Phys. Rev. B 29, 1685 (1984).
Vitor M. Pereira, Johan Nilsson, and A. H. Castro Neto, Phys.Rev.Lett.99, 166802 (2007).
Yu. N. Demkov and V. N. Ostrovskii, SOVIET PHYSICS JETP 13,1083-1087,(1971).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Handbook of Mathematical Functions with Formulas, Graphs, and Tables

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Handbook of Mathematical Functions with Formulas,Graphs, and Tables.ed. by Milton Abramowitz and Irene A.Stegun,National Bureau of Standards, Washington DC,(1964).
M.Onoda, S.Murakami and N.Nagaosa, Phys. Rev. Lett. 93, 083901,(2004).
A. Manchon, H.C. Koo, J. Nitta, S.M. Frolov, R.A. Duine,Nature Materials, 14,871–882 (2015).
